_meta.how_to_regress: npm run check:golden leverage-logic  (dev backend up)
_meta.note: debrief case guards clean outcome_grade / value_left enums (were inverted by annotations).

input.situation: Asking for a raise after two years without one; I lead the team that shipped our biggest feature this year and I have a competing offer at 15% more.
input.leverage: Competing offer, strong performance reviews, only person who knows the billing system
input.desired: A 12-15% raise or a promotion with comp review

situation_read.summary: Two years of pay stagnation against a backdrop of leading the company's biggest feature ship this year creates a textbook leverage moment, amplified by a live competing offer and unique institutional knowledge of the billing system.
situation_read.power_explanation: The competing offer sets a market floor, the billing system dependency creates replacement risk, and two years without a raise gives moral weight to the ask.

your_leverage.point: A real competing offer at 15% more sets an external market price for your work that the company must beat or match.
your_leverage.how_to_use: Reference it as market data, not a threat — frame it as 'I want to stay, but I need this to make sense financially.'
your_leverage.when: Middle — after establishing your performance case, before they stall.
your_leverage.warning: Leading with it reads as ultimatum and can make the conversation adversarial before it needs to be.

your_leverage.point: You are the sole owner of the billing system, making your immediate departure operationally disruptive and expensive to replace.
your_leverage.how_to_use: Let this sit implicit — they know it; mentioning it subtly ('I'd want to make sure whoever takes billing handoff is set up well') signals the risk without being coercive.
your_leverage.when: Only if they push back hard or stall — it's a silent pressure point.
your_leverage.warning: Saying it out loud can feel like a hostage-taking move and damage the relationship you need to preserve.

your_leverage.point: You shipped the company's highest-impact feature this year with documented strong performance reviews.
your_leverage.how_to_use: Open with specific results — revenue impact, users affected, timeline delivered — not adjectives.
your_leverage.when: Early — establish the value anchor before any number is on the table.
your_leverage.warning: Don't over-rehearse it; reciting metrics robotically undercuts the credibility of the achievement.

your_leverage.point: Two years without a raise means inflation alone has cut your real compensation, giving the ask a fairness framing that's hard to argue against.
your_leverage.how_to_use: Mention it once as context — 'My comp hasn't moved in two years while my scope has grown significantly' — then drop it.
your_leverage.when: Early, paired with the performance case to show value growth outpacing pay.
your_leverage.warning: Dwelling on it shifts the tone from forward-looking to grievance, which weakens your position.

their_leverage.point: Budget cycles and approval chains mean even a willing manager may not be able to move fast.
their_leverage.how_to_neutralize: Ask directly what the approval process looks like and set a specific follow-up date so 'I need to check' doesn't become indefinite delay.

their_leverage.point: They can counter that the competing offer comes with unknowns — different culture, risk, ramp-up time — to reduce its urgency.
their_leverage.how_to_neutralize: Acknowledge that you prefer to stay, which is why you're having the conversation, but be clear the offer is real and the deadline is real.

their_leverage.point: Promotion decisions often require a case built over time, giving them a reason to defer comp review to a future cycle.
their_leverage.how_to_neutralize: Come in with both options framed — raise now, or promotion-track commitment with a comp review date locked in — so delay requires actively rejecting a path, not just saying 'not yet.'

strategy.approach: Performance-anchored ask with market validation, keeping the relationship intact while making inaction costly.
strategy.opening_position: Ask for 15% to anchor at the top of your range and leave room to land at 12-13% while still winning.
strategy.target: 15% raise or a promotion with a committed comp review bringing you to at least 12% within 60 days.
strategy.walkaway: Below 12% with no promotion path or title change, the competing offer becomes the right move.

concession_ladder.give_up: Flexibility on the effective date — accepting a raise that starts 30 days out instead of immediately.
concession_ladder.value_to_them: high — gives them time to process payroll and approvals without losing the negotiation

concession_ladder.give_up: Accepting 12% now with a written 90-day review committed to hitting 15% upon a defined milestone.
concession_ladder.value_to_them: medium — splits the ask across time, which is easier to approve in stages

strategy.timing: Request the meeting this week — competing offer timelines are real and waiting past the holiday weekend signals low urgency.
strategy.setting: In person or video one-on-one with your direct manager only — no email for the core ask, and never loop in HR before your manager hears it first.

scripts.say_this: Hey, I wanted to carve out some time to talk about where I am — my role has grown a lot, especially this year, and I think it's the right moment to revisit my comp.
scripts.why_it_works: It's direct without being combative and frames the conversation as a natural next step, not a confrontation.

scripts.say_this: We shipped [feature name], which is the biggest thing this team has delivered — I led that end to end, my reviews have been strong, and I haven't had a comp adjustment in two years, so I'm asking for a 15% increase.
scripts.why_it_works: Stacks the evidence before the number so the ask feels earned, not arbitrary.

scripts.moment: If they say no or push back
scripts.say_this: I hear you on the constraints — help me understand what path gets us there, because I genuinely want to make this work here.
scripts.why_it_works: Turns pushback into a problem-solving moment and keeps you on the same side of the table as your manager.

scripts.say_this: I want to be transparent with you — I've been approached by another company and the offer is meaningful, which is part of why I wanted to have this conversation now rather than just sit on it.
scripts.why_it_works: Introduces the competing offer as honesty, not a threat, which respects the relationship while making the stakes real.

scripts.say_this: So can we land on either 15% effective the first of next month, or a promotion track with a committed review date — I'm flexible on the structure, I just need something concrete today.
scripts.why_it_works: Gives them two yes options instead of yes or no, and 'concrete today' prevents an open-ended stall.

traps_to_avoid.trap: Revealing the exact competing company or offer letter details when asked.
traps_to_avoid.why_dangerous: They can research the company, decide the role doesn't fit you, or use details to slow-play until the offer expires.
traps_to_avoid.instead: Confirm the offer is real and the number is real — 'I'd rather not share specifics, but it's a serious offer at a meaningful number' — and hold there.

traps_to_avoid.trap: Accepting a vague 'we'll revisit this at your next review' as an outcome.
traps_to_avoid.why_dangerous: Without a date, a number, and a written record, it's a non-commitment that resets you to square one in six months.
traps_to_avoid.instead: If they offer a future review, pin it to a specific date, a specific range, and ask for it in a follow-up email you send that afternoon.

traps_to_avoid.trap: Negotiating against yourself by softening the ask before they've even responded.
traps_to_avoid.why_dangerous: Saying 'I was thinking maybe 10 or 12%' before they push back gives up ground you hadn't even lost yet.
traps_to_avoid.instead: State 15%, then go silent — let them respond to the number before you move anything.

traps_to_avoid.trap: Letting the conversation turn into a performance review instead of a compensation decision.
traps_to_avoid.why_dangerous: If they pivot to discussing your areas for growth, the frame shifts from 'you've earned this' to 'let's see if you deserve it,' and you lose the initiative.
traps_to_avoid.instead: Acknowledge any feedback briefly and redirect — 'Happy to dig into that separately — for today, I want to stay focused on the comp question.'

batna.best_alternative: The competing offer at 15% more, which you can accept if this negotiation fails to hit your walkaway of 12%.
batna.how_to_strengthen: Before the meeting, call the competing company and confirm the offer is still live and the timeline — you need to know exactly how long you have before the window closes.

do: Sit forward slightly when presenting your ask — it projects ownership of the conversation without aggression.
Pause after stating your number and hold eye contact — silence after an anchor is a feature, not awkward.
Nod once when they speak — signals you're listening, not waiting to rebut.
avoid: Don't look away or down when you say the number — breaking eye contact reads as uncertainty about your own ask.
Don't touch your face or neck when they push back — it signals stress and undermines the confident tone you're building.
Don't lean back in your chair when they speak — it reads as disengagement or defensiveness.

output.confidence_boost: You walked in with a live offer, sole ownership of a critical system, and the biggest ship of the year on your record — they need this conversation to go well more than you do.

input.situation: Salary negotiation: asked for 15% with a competing offer in hand.
input.strategy: Lead with the competing offer, anchor at 15%, concession ladder to 10% + title bump.

timeline.what: Presented the ask at 15% with market data
Manager said budget is frozen until Q3 but offered 8% now

input.finalOutcome: Got 8% now plus a written commitment to revisit at 12% in Q3 with a title bump.
input.desiredOutcome: A 12-15% raise or a promotion with comp review

output.outcome_summary: Landed 8% immediately with a binding Q3 path to 12% and a title bump — within the target range but back-loaded and contingent.

what_worked.tactic: Anchoring at 15% with market data created room to negotiate down while still landing above the midpoint floor.
what_worked.impact: Preserved 8% now instead of settling at the first counteroffer of something lower.

what_worked.tactic: Holding the competing offer as leverage forced a written Q3 commitment rather than a vague 'we'll revisit it' promise.
what_worked.impact: Converted a verbal budget excuse into a documented obligation with a specific quarter attached.

what_didnt.mistake: Conceding to the Q3 timeline without negotiating the trigger conditions or consequences if the 12% review is delayed or softened.
what_didnt.cost: If Q3 comes and budget is 'frozen again,' there's no enforcement mechanism — effectively risked 4% with no recourse.
what_didnt.next_time: Before accepting any deferred comp structure, specify in writing what happens if the review doesn't result in 12% — tie it to a resignation right or formal escalation clause.

what_didnt.mistake: The negotiation ended in two rounds with no counter to the 8% offer before accepting the split structure.
what_didnt.cost: Skipping a counter on the immediate number likely cost 1-2% upfront — the gap between 8% and a possible 9-10% was never tested.
what_didnt.next_time: Always counter at least once before accepting a split deal; propose 10% now plus Q3 title review as a middle position.

value_left_on_table.explanation: The Q3 commitment is only as strong as the manager's authority and the company's Q3 budget reality — if either slips, the delta between 8% and 12% (roughly 4% of salary, potentially $4K-$8K depending on base) evaporates. The immediate number was also likely soft.
value_left_on_table.how_to_capture: Before Q3 arrives, schedule a formal check-in in writing for mid-Q3, confirm the title change paperwork is initiated now, and use any competing offers that surface before then as a re-opener.

leverage_lessons: A competing offer is a time-sensitive asset — its power decays the longer the negotiation drags or the more it's implied rather than presented with a deadline.
A frozen budget is a constraint, not a ceiling — the manager found 8% inside a 'frozen' budget, which means the ceiling was higher than disclosed.
Written commitments are only as strong as their specificity — 'revisit at 12%' is not the same as 'you will receive 12%.'

for_next_time.do_more: Use the competing offer to impose a decision deadline, not just an anchor — 'I need to respond to the other offer by Friday' forces a real decision.
for_next_time.do_less: Stop accepting deferred structures without locking down the conditions, triggers, and consequences in the same document.
for_next_time.try_new: Introduce a signing-bonus ask as a bridge when immediate salary is constrained — it bypasses budget-cycle friction and can close the gap between 8% now and 12% later in a single lump sum.
for_next_time.key_insight: You won the structure of the deal but gave away enforcement — the next negotiation is actually Q3, and you need to prepare for it now.
